bids.reasoning: This is the opening bid at t=0, so I should start with my maximum utility offer. My most important issue is issueE (0.3 weight), followed by issueB and issueD (0.2 each), then issueA and issueC (0.1 each). For all issues, valueE gives the highest utility (1.0), so I'll bid all valueE to maximize my utility at the start of the negotiation.

It's very early (t=0.02), so I have plenty of time to negotiate. My opening was all valueE (maximum utility). The opponent's offer gives me low utility: issueA=valueA(0.20), issueB=valueA(0.20), issueC=valueB(0.73), issueD=valueA(0.42), issueE=valueB(0.31) - this is clearly not balanced despite their claim. I'll make a slight concession from my opening on lower-weight issues (issueA, issueC) while holding firm on high-weight issues (issueE, issueB, issueD).

The opponent's offers are extremely low utility for me - their latest bid (issueA: valueA, issueB: valueA, issueC: valueB, issueD: valueA, issueE: valueA) gives me very poor utility. They claim to have made concessions but actually moved issueE from valueB to valueA (worse for me), which contradicts their claim of conceding. We're only at t=0.04, so there's no need to concede much yet. I'll maintain a high-utility bid but make a small concession from my opening all-valueE bid, particularly on lower-weight issues like issueA and issueC.

The opponent is essentially holding firm on their position (issueA: valueA, issueB: valueA, issueC: valueB, issueD: valueA, issueE: valueA or valueB) despite claiming concessions - their bids actually got worse from t=0.02 to t=0.04. Their messages are classic deceptive cooperation tactics. We're very early in the negotiation (t=0.06), so I should stay near my high-utility bids. My most important issues are issueE (0.3), issueB (0.2), and issueD (0.2). I'll make a small concession on low-weight issues (issueA, issueC) while holding firm on my high-weight issues.

The opponent is clearly not conceding at all - their offers have been essentially the same (all valueA/low values) across every turn, despite claiming they're making "meaningful concessions." Their messages are deceptive cheap talk. I'm only at t=0.08, so there's plenty of time. I should continue holding firm on my high-weight issues (issueE weight 0.3, issueB and issueD weight 0.2 each) while making minor concessions on low-weight issues. I'll keep issueE at valueE, issueD at valueE, issueB at valueE, and try slight variations on lower-weight issues (issueA, issueC) to show some movement without sacrificing much utility.

The opponent has been nearly immovable, consistently bidding valueA for issueA, valueA for issueB, valueB for issueC, and valueA for issueD, with only small changes on issueE (valueB → valueA → valueA → valueA → valueB) and issueD (now valueC). Their latest offer gives me very low utility. My most important issue is issueE (0.3 weight) where I want high values, and issueB/issueD (0.2 each). The opponent's messages are pure cheap talk - they claim concessions but barely move. I should hold firm on my high-weight issues (issueE, issueB, issueD) while I can make small concessions on lower-weight issues (issueA, issueC). At t=0.1 we're still early, so I'll maintain a strong position but vary slightly from my last bid to avoid repetition.
